template.heading: Industry intelligence by Configure One
template.description: We have a curated knowledge base of terminology, product patterns, rules, BOM patterns, pitfalls, and more for your industry to improve product modeling quality. Review this excerpt for relevance.
template.excerpt:
Vocational truck bodies (service, dump, utility, van, refrigerated), trailers (flatbed, tanker, dry van, refrigerated, lowboy, dump, specialty), van upfits (contractor, mobile service, ambulance, shuttle), and vehicle accessories (lift gates, toolboxes, cranes, hitches). Body and upfit configuration is chassis-dependent — the Ford, GM, Ram, Freightliner, and Kenworth chassis sets the physical and regulatory starting point.

Sold through vocational truck dealers and body distributors, typically as a multi-stage build: chassis sold by OEM dealer, body built by a separate manufacturer, upfit by a third stage. Fleet and single-unit sales are both common. End customer is rarely the same as the chassis buyer.

Chassis-dependent configure-to-order is the dominant pattern. The chassis is selected with defined wheelbase, cab-to-axle, GVWR, and other fixed specs; the body and upfit configuration must fit within those specs. Weight budgets (GVWR minus curb weight equals payload) are the primary physical constraint.

Key terminology includes GVWR (Gross Vehicle Weight Rating), GAWR (Gross Axle Weight Rating), curb weight, payload, wheelbase, cab-to-axle, PTO (Power Take-Off), FMVSS (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards), QVM (Qualified Vehicle Modifier), and BBLB (Body Builder Layout Book).

One Product per body type or upfit platform. The chassis is an Input, not a Product. Multi-stage builds use nested configurations. Driven Inputs handle chassis-to-body compatibility, Product Equations handle weight-budget calculations, and BOM Logic handles accessory content.

Common pitfalls include treating the chassis as a Product instead of an Input, treating GVWR as display-only instead of enforcing the payload budget, ignoring PTO as a chassis factory-option dependency, and not modeling the multi-stage certification chain for FMVSS compliance.
